label: SES-073 PI-045 step-5 conversation: Service Token rotation completed, mcp-secret persistence completed, end-to-end auth verification through the tunnel completed — then claude.ai connector registration discovered claude.ai's UI supports only OAuth 2.1 (no custom HTTP headers), making the X-CRMBuilder-Secret + Cloudflare Service Token model from DEC-204/225 incompatible. DEC-226 reroutes the auth model to OAuth 2.1 + PKCE with a timeboxed Path B (Cloudflare Access as OIDC IdP) feasibility check before committing to Path A (server-side OAuth). PI-049 created with four-phase implementation scope. WT-046 carries the kickoff body inline (dogfooding the v0.7 governance pattern of database-backed kickoffs). Records originally landed via direct-API POSTs from a small helper script — ses_073.json + this deposit_event backfill the archival audit chain. 1 session, 1 decision, 1 planning item, 1 work_ticket, 7 references.

session.title: PI-045 step-5 conversation: Service Token rotation + mcp-secret persistence completed, but claude.ai connector registration found to be incompatible with header-based auth; DEC-226 reroutes to OAuth 2.1 + PI-049 created to enable claude.ai integration
session.conversation_reference: Claude Code session at Doug's terminal, 2026-05-24 evening through 2026-05-25 early morning UTC, opened against the PI-045 step-5 kickoff file PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-step-5-claude-ai-mcp-integration-registration-kickoff.md.
session.topics_covered: Service Token rotation (claude-ai-mcp Client ID rotated to 39ac09d4d7c707c75c2b9187a896d67c.access; Client Secret rotated; files at ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-{id,secret} updated; password manager updated; end-to-end Cloudflare Access verification curl returned HTTP 502 confirming the new token is accepted at the edge and traffic routed to the tunnel target). mcp-secret persistence (discovered missing — SES-072's setup had only set the value as an ephemeral env var; new 64-char hex secret generated via secrets.token_hex(32) and written to ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret mode 0600, also saved to password manager). MCP HTTP transport startup (uv run crmbuilder-v2-mcp --transport streamable-http with CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET sourced from the new file; Uvicorn confirmed listening on 127.0.0.1:8810; SharedSecretMiddleware initialized cleanly). End-to-end auth verification through the tunnel (curl with all three custom headers returned HTTP 404 from the MCP transport — Cloudflare Access accepted the Service Token (CF_Authorization JWT issued with common_name = 39ac09d4d7c707c75c2b9187a896d67c.access matching the new Client ID), tunnel routed, SharedSecretMiddleware accepted X-CRMBuilder-Secret, MCP transport answered with 404 because GET / isn't a route it serves — all four layers cooperated). claude.ai connector registration (navigated to Settings → Connectors → + Add connector → Add custom connector form with Name + Remote MCP server URL + Advanced settings collapsed; Advanced settings exposed only OAuth Client ID + OAuth Client Secret — no custom header configuration; alternative Browse Connector option is the marketplace, not self-hosted). Research into claude.ai's connector auth model (WebFetch of Anthropic support docs + WebSearch confirming claude.ai's connectors use OAuth 2.1 + PKCE per MCP spec; GitHub issue #112 'Cannot configure Authorization: Bearer for custom remote MCP' confirms custom HTTP headers are an open feature request not supported today). Three-path analysis (Path A: implement OAuth on our server, multi-slice work; Path B: use Cloudflare Access as OAuth IdP, less work if feasible; Path C: defer claude.ai integration, rejected as impractical because cut/paste friction is what PI-045 was created to eliminate). DEC-226 (replace X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware with OAuth 2.1; timeboxed Path B feasibility check first). PI-049 created (the OAuth implementation workstream). Direct-API writes via inline Python script of DEC-226 / PI-049 / SES-073 / WT-046 / CONV-046 plus wiring references — dogfooding the v0.7 governance entity model for session-to-session handoff (kickoff body stored in WT-046 rather than in a markdown file).
session.summary: PI-045 step 5 reached the claude.ai connector registration step and discovered that claude.ai's UI supports only OAuth 2.1 auth — no custom HTTP headers — making the X-CRMBuilder-Secret + Cloudflare Service Token model from DEC-204/225 incompatible. The rotation + mcp-secret persistence work completed cleanly (and end-to-end verified through the tunnel), but registration is blocked. DEC-226 settles the architectural reroute to OAuth 2.1 + PKCE; PI-049 captures the implementation scope with a timeboxed Path B (Cloudflare Access as OIDC IdP) feasibility check before committing to Path A (OAuth on our server). PI-045 is blocked by PI-049. Lesson: verify the integrating client's UI auth model before designing server-side auth that must plug into it.

New mcp-secret persisted at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret 64-char hex mode 0600 (filesystem-level; not git-tracked; will be removed in PI-049 Phase 3). DEC-226 (in-database; this session's settled decision). PI-049 (in-database; new planning item for OAuth implementation). WT-046 (in-database; kickoff prompt for the PI-049 conversation, with full body inline in work_ticket_description). CONV-046 (in-database; the planned next conversation entity, status=ready). Reference edges (DEC-226 decided_in SES-073; DEC-226 references DEC-225; DEC-226 references DEC-204; SES-073 is_about PI-045; SES-073 is_about PI-049; PI-045 blocked_by PI-049; WT-046 addresses PI-049; CONV-046 conversation_records_session SES-073's successor; CONV-046 opens_against_work_ticket WT-046; CONV-046 addresses PI-049). Two-day-old Claude Code feedback memory feedback_one_step_at_a_time.md (operational walkthroughs at Doug's terminal: one command per turn, wait for output before next).
session.in_flight_at_end: PI-049 conversation needs to be opened — Phase 1 (Path B feasibility) is the first work, timeboxed 60 minutes. The launch prompt for the new conversation is in WT-046's work_ticket_description (read it via the v2 MCP server's get_work_ticket tool or curl /work-tickets/WT-046 from the new session). The MCP HTTP transport is currently running at 127.0.0.1:8810 in a terminal on Doug's host; it can be killed (it's vestigial for the OAuth path) or left running for curl debugging. The Service Token credentials remain in place — vestigial for claude.ai-via-OAuth but useful for curl/admin testing and defense-in-depth. mcp-secret file remains in place until PI-049 Phase 3 cleanup. PI-045 status remains Open with the blocked_by edge to PI-049 carrying the dependency semantics.

decisions.title: v2 MCP server auth model: replace shared-secret + Cloudflare Service Token with OAuth 2.1 + PKCE to enable claude.ai connector registration
decisions.context: PI-045 step 5 reached the claude.ai connector UI on 2026-05-25 and discovered that claude.ai's 'Add custom connector' form supports only OAuth 2.1 (Client ID + Client Secret in Advanced Settings) — no custom HTTP header configuration. The existing auth architecture (DEC-204 dual-layer + DEC-225 Service Token + X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware from SES-072 slice B) cannot be configured into claude.ai's UI. Per Anthropic's connector documentation and the MCP 2.1 spec, claude.ai's connector model is OAuth 2.1 authorization_code with PKCE; the Advanced Settings OAuth Client ID/Secret fields are for the 'Anthropic-held credentials' registration mode. DEC-225's premise that claude.ai's MCP integration cannot follow OAuth redirects was either time-bound (true at SES-065 authoring, now superseded by Anthropic's connector evolution) or scope-bound (true for some MCP transport variant but not the connector path). GitHub issue #112 (anthropics/claude-ai-mcp, 'Cannot configure Authorization: Bearer for custom remote MCP') confirms custom HTTP header auth is not supported and is an open feature request without a committed timeline. End-to-end auth verified working in this conversation prior to discovery — the Service Token rotation completed cleanly, the missing mcp-secret file was generated and persisted (SES-072's setup had set the value as an ephemeral env var only), and a curl test through the full stack returned HTTP 404 from the MCP transport (Cloudflare Access accepted the new Service Token, tunnel routed, SharedSecretMiddleware accepted the header, MCP transport answered with 404 for GET / — all four layers cooperated). The dead end is at the registration step, not anywhere in the auth chain we built.
decisions.decision: Replace the X-CRMBuilder-Secret SharedSecretMiddleware (added in SES-072 slice B) with an OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation on the v2 MCP HTTP server. Concretely: (a) serve Protected Resource Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource (RFC 9728) declaring the authorization server URI, supported scopes, and bearer token methods; (b) implement the authorization server endpoints (/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server, /authorize, /token, optionally /register for Dynamic Client Registration) — OR delegate to an external authorization server if Path B (Cloudflare Access as OIDC IdP) is feasible; (c) bearer-token validation middleware on the MCP routes; (d) token issuance via authorization_code grant with PKCE verification. The Cloudflare Access edge (Service Token policy + One-time PIN admin policy from DEC-225) remains in place for non-claude.ai access and for defense-in-depth. The decision between Path A (implement OAuth ourselves) and Path B (delegate to Cloudflare Access) is timeboxed at the start of PI-049's conversation: up to 60 minutes investigating Path B feasibility (does Cloudflare Access serve MCP-spec-compliant Protected Resource Metadata? does claude.ai accept its issuer/audience values?) before committing to Path A. Path C (defer claude.ai integration entirely, keep header-based auth, use Claude Code only) was rejected as impractical — the cut/paste friction between Claude Code and claude.ai is what PI-045 was created to eliminate.
decisions.rationale: claude.ai's connector UI is the gating constraint, and it speaks OAuth 2.1 only. No amount of server-side header-based auth makes the connector usable. OAuth is also more MCP-spec-compliant — Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) and OAuth 2.1 with PKCE are the MCP spec's prescribed model for remote authorization. The shared-secret + Service Token architecture from DEC-204/225 was a useful intermediate state that proved out Cloudflare Tunnel + Cloudflare Access for the remote-access deliverable, and the Cloudflare Access edge stays in place. The middleware swap (X-CRMBuilder-Secret → bearer-token) is a discrete code change, not an architectural reset.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Path B (use Cloudflare Access as OAuth IdP — claude.ai's OAuth fields point at Cloudflare's OAuth endpoints; we serve only Protected Resource Metadata pointing at Cloudflare) is held open as a timeboxed feasibility check before committing to Path A; less code if it works, unknown whether Cloudflare's OAuth implementation satisfies the MCP spec exactly. Path C (defer claude.ai integration entirely, keep header-based auth, ship Claude Code + desktop UI only) was rejected — defeats the cut/paste-elimination purpose of PI-045. Path D (add an external OAuth provider like Auth0, Stytch, WorkOS, Logto) was considered but adds an external dependency and likely a recurring cost for a single-user system.
decisions.consequences: PI-045 step 5 is blocked until PI-049 lands (captured as a PI-045 -[blocked_by]-> PI-049 reference). SES-072's slice B (X-CRMBuilder-Secret SharedSecretMiddleware) and the mcp-secret persistence (file at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret) become obsolete on PI-049 Phase 3 cleanup. The Service Token rotation completed in this conversation was wasted work for the claude.ai path but not damaging — rotation is good hygiene regardless, and the Service Token stays in place for curl tests and defense-in-depth. The Cloudflare Tunnel + Access infrastructure (SES-071's deployment) remains useful; the Service Token specifically becomes vestigial once claude.ai goes through OAuth, but stays for admin paths. The slice C marker-drift guard from SES-072 is engagement-agnostic and orthogonal to auth — stays in place unchanged. Lesson — verify the claude.ai connector UI's auth model BEFORE designing backend auth that has to integrate with it. This belongs in PI-049's close-out as a methodology feedback entry, and in Claude Code's user memory as a feedback memory for future architecture-before-integration work.

planning_items.title: v2 MCP server OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation: enable claude.ai custom connector registration by replacing the X-CRMBuilder-Secret shared-secret middleware with OAuth-token-based auth (with a timeboxed Path B feasibility check before committing to Path A)
planning_items.description:
Scope per DEC-226. Four phases.

PHASE 1 — Path B feasibility (timebox 60 minutes). Investigate whether Cloudflare Access can be configured as an OIDC IdP that claude.ai's connector accepts: (a) read Cloudflare's SaaS Application docs and OIDC documentation; (b) verify whether Cloudflare exposes MCP-spec-compliant Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) or whether we'd need to serve our own pointing at Cloudflare's authorization endpoints; (c) identify the issuer/audience values claude.ai expects vs. what Cloudflare emits; (d) check whether Cloudflare's OAuth flow supports PKCE per the MCP 2.1 spec. If Path B is feasible within reasonable Cloudflare-config work, commit to Path B and SKIP Phase 2's implementation. If Path B is not feasible or fails the timebox, proceed to Phase 2.

PHASE 2 — Path A implementation (only if Path B doesn't pan out). Implement OAuth 2.1 + PKCE on the v2 MCP server: (1) Protected Resource Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource per RFC 9728 (authorization server URI, supported scopes, bearer token methods); (2) Authorization Server Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server with /authorize and /token endpoints; (3) /authorize endpoint with minimal consent UI (single-user system; consent UI can be terse — 'Allow claude.ai to access CRMBuilder v2 governance database? Yes/No'); (4) /token endpoint with code-for-token exchange and PKCE verification; (5) bearer-token validation middleware on MCP routes (replaces SharedSecretMiddleware from SES-072 slice B); (6) token storage in v2.db (new tables: oauth_clients, oauth_authorization_codes, oauth_access_tokens — schema designed in the conversation); (7) optionally /register for Dynamic Client Registration (defer if Anthropic-held credentials registration mode works fine — claude.ai's Advanced Settings already accepts pre-registered Client ID/Secret).

PHASE 3 — Cleanup (both paths). Remove the SharedSecretMiddleware from slice B; remove mcp-secret persistence (file at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret, env var CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET, the startup hard-fail check); update docs to reflect the new auth model; remove X-CRMBuilder-Secret guidance from the PI-045 step-5 kickoff and any other kickoff files; rotate-and-archive the Service Token credentials (keep in place but document as defense-in-depth, not the primary auth path); update CLAUDE.md's PI-045/auth-model sections.

PHASE 4 — claude.ai connector registration (the PI-045 step-5 work that this PI unblocks). Register against the new OAuth-enabled mcp.crmbuilder.ai endpoint; verify all 44 MCP tools discoverable; run the smoke test per the original PI-045 step-5 kickoff (read surface + write surface; engagement marker drives routing; three auth-path negative cases); ship the documentation deliverables per PI-045 part (d).

OUT OF SCOPE: replacing the Cloudflare Tunnel or Cloudflare Access edge (both stay); expanding to multi-user (single-user system; consent UI is minimal); persistent token storage with refresh tokens (a single long-lived bearer per claude.ai connection is sufficient — re-auth on expiry is acceptable for a single-user system).

On PI-049 completion, PI-045 step 5 + smoke test + documentation can execute in a follow-up conversation (or be folded into PI-049's Phase 4).

work_tickets.work_ticket_title: Kickoff: PI-049 OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation for v2 MCP server (timeboxed Path B feasibility first)
work_tickets.work_ticket_description:
# Kickoff: PI-049 — v2 MCP server OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation

**Status:** Ready (work_ticket_status=ready). This kickoff body lives inline in WT-046's `work_ticket_description` (dogfooding the v0.7 governance entity model — no separate markdown file). The new conversation reads this via `get_work_ticket("WT-046")` over MCP or `GET /work-tickets/WT-046` over REST.

**Anticipated session at close of the new conversation:** SES-074 (subject to identifier rebasing per the recent parallel-sandbox pattern; verify with `list_recent_sessions` at conversation open).

---

## Read first (v2 orientation, MCP-connected session)

1. `get_current_status()` — current v2 governance state.
2. `get_decision("DEC-226")` — the decision that motivated this PI. Read fully.
3. `get_planning_item("PI-049")` — this PI's scope (four-phase plan).
4. `get_session("SES-073")` — the dead-end conversation that led to DEC-226 and PI-049. Read `topics_covered`, `summary`, `artifacts_produced`, `in_flight_at_end`.
5. `get_planning_item("PI-045")` — the upstream remote-access workstream; read for context. PI-045 is blocked by PI-049.
6. `get_decision("DEC-204")` — the dual-layer architecture (Cloudflare Tunnel + Cloudflare Access + app-layer auth).
7. `get_decision("DEC-225")` — the Cloudflare Access two-policy split (Service Token + One-time PIN). DEC-226 revises but does not supersede this.
8. `get_decision("DEC-205")` — the engagement-marker fail-loud guard from SES-072 slice C. Orthogonal to auth; stays unchanged.
9. Also read `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` (the project root CLAUDE.md, automatic on session open).

## Read first (file-fallback if MCP not connected)

Read `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/` snapshots: `decisions.json`, `planning_items.json`, `sessions.json`, `references.json`. The seven records named above are all in the snapshots once the db-export regeneration runs.

---

## Phase 1 — Path B feasibility investigation (timeboxed 60 minutes)

Investigate whether Cloudflare Access can be configured as an OIDC IdP that claude.ai's connector accepts. Specific questions to answer:

- Does Cloudflare Access support being configured as an OAuth/OIDC SaaS application that issues bearer tokens claude.ai's MCP connector can use?
- Can Cloudflare emit MCP-spec-compliant Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) at a standard `/.well-known/` path on `mcp.crmbuilder.ai`, OR do we need to serve our own metadata document pointing at Cloudflare's OAuth endpoints?
- What issuer/audience values does claude.ai's MCP client expect? Does Cloudflare's OAuth implementation match?
- Does Cloudflare's OAuth flow support PKCE (mandatory per MCP 2.1 spec)?
- What's the actual cost (in Cloudflare config + minimal server work) of standing this up?

If Path B is feasible within reasonable work and survives the timebox: commit to Path B, skip Phase 2, go directly to Phase 3 cleanup + Phase 4 registration.

If Path B is infeasible or unclear after the timebox: commit to Path A. Capture the Path B investigation findings as a decision (DEC-227 candidate: "Path B infeasible because <reasons>; PI-049 proceeds with Path A") so the rationale is preserved.

## Phase 2 — Path A implementation (only if Path B doesn't pan out)

Implement OAuth 2.1 + PKCE on the v2 MCP server. Seven sub-deliverables; the conversation may slice them differently:

1. Protected Resource Metadata at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` per RFC 9728 (authorization server URI, supported scopes, bearer token methods).
2. Authorization Server Metadata at `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` with `/authorize` and `/token` endpoints.
3. `/authorize` endpoint with minimal consent UI (single-user system; consent UI can be terse — "Allow claude.ai to access CRMBuilder v2 governance database? Yes/No").
4. `/token` endpoint with code-for-token exchange and PKCE verification.
5. Bearer-token validation middleware on MCP routes (replaces `SharedSecretMiddleware` from SES-072 slice B).
6. Token storage in `v2.db` — new tables `oauth_clients`, `oauth_authorization_codes`, `oauth_access_tokens` (schema designed in the conversation; lean — single-user system, no refresh tokens needed).
7. Optionally `/register` for Dynamic Client Registration (defer if Anthropic-held credentials registration mode works fine — claude.ai's Advanced Settings already accepts pre-registered Client ID/Secret).

Slice these so each is testable end-to-end before the next lands. Suggested order: 5 (middleware skeleton accepting any Bearer for fast iteration) → 1+2 (well-known endpoints) → 6 (storage) → 3+4 (authorize + token) → 7 (DCR) if pursued.

## Phase 3 — Cleanup (both paths)

- Remove `SharedSecretMiddleware` from `crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/mcp_server/middleware.py`.
- Remove `CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET` env var, the startup hard-fail check, and the `~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret` file persistence.
- Update docs to reflect the OAuth model: `crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md` (already in PI-045 step-5 scope; expand here) and `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` PI-045/auth-model section.
- Remove X-CRMBuilder-Secret guidance from `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-step-5-claude-ai-mcp-integration-registration-kickoff.md`.
- Decide: keep or rotate the Service Token? Recommend keep (defense-in-depth + curl/admin testing path; documented as not the primary auth path).

## Phase 4 — claude.ai connector registration (the original PI-045 step-5 work, now unblocked)

- Register against the new OAuth-enabled `mcp.crmbuilder.ai` endpoint.
- Verify all 44 MCP tools discoverable.
- Run the smoke test per the original PI-045 step-5 kickoff: read surface (`get_current_charter`, `get_current_status`, `list_decisions_for_session`, etc.); write surface (`create_decision`, `replace_charter`, `add_reference`, `update_planning_item`, etc.); engagement-marker-drives-routing confirmation; three auth-path negative cases (no creds → CF Access rejects; valid CF but invalid OAuth → 401; valid auth + marker drift → 409).
- Ship the documentation deliverables per PI-045 part (d).

---

## Out of scope (explicit)

- Replacing the Cloudflare Tunnel or Cloudflare Access edge — both stay. Cloudflare Access continues to gate the hostname; the OAuth flow runs inside it.
- Expanding to multi-user — single-user system; consent UI is minimal.
- Persistent token storage with refresh tokens — a single long-lived bearer per claude.ai connection is sufficient; re-auth on expiry is acceptable for a single-user system.
- Implementing OIDC ID tokens — pure OAuth 2.1 access tokens are sufficient.

---

## Folding into PI-045 closure

On PI-049 completion (Phase 4 ships), PI-045's status transitions Open → Resolved with `resolution_reference` pointing at the SES that closed PI-049. The remaining PI-045 step-5 deliverables (smoke test + documentation) are folded into Phase 4 above per the original PI-045 step-5 kickoff's folding rationale.

## Lesson to capture in the close-out

**Verify the integrating client's UI auth model before designing server-side auth that must plug into it.** This is the methodological lesson from SES-073. The PI-045 + SES-071 + SES-072 chain built a complete auth stack (Cloudflare Tunnel + Service Token + Cloudflare Access + X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware) without ever inspecting what claude.ai's connector UI actually accepts. The discovery at the registration step cost several conversations of work. Capture this as a methodology feedback entry in the SES-074 close-out, and as a Claude Code feedback memory for future architecture-before-integration work.

---

## Identifier note

This kickoff anticipates SES-074. Verify identifier heads with `list_recent_sessions` at conversation open; rebase pre-emptively if heads have advanced. PI-032's reserve-at-apply-time identifier model would eliminate this class of collision.

## Working pattern

Operating mode: **DETAIL** for the Phase 1 investigation (specific questions to answer, specific docs to read), then **ARCHITECTURE** for Phase 2 implementation slicing. Doug runs every executable step at his terminal and in the Cloudflare dashboard; Claude provides bash/curl blocks and architectural framing. Per the SES-073 feedback memory `feedback_one_step_at_a_time.md`: when at Doug's terminal, one command/action per turn, wait for output.

## Deliverable shape

Triple-artifact close-out per the SES-046–SES-052 precedent:
1. `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_074.json`
2. `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-074.md`
3. Code commits (the OAuth implementation, if Path A is taken) + doc updates per Phase 3.
